items.explanation: Stops the current generation immediately. Useful when Claude is producing too much output or going in the wrong direction.
Press Ctrl+C twice quickly to force quit Claude Code entirely.
Clears the current conversation display while keeping context. Good for visual cleanup.
Sends end-of-file signal to exit the Claude Code CLI gracefully.

items.description: Move cursor to start of line
items.explanation: Standard terminal shortcut — jumps cursor to the beginning of the current input line.

items.description: Move cursor to end of line
items.explanation: Standard terminal shortcut — jumps cursor to the end of the current input line.
Clears everything from cursor position to the beginning of the line.
Clears everything from cursor position to the end of the line.
Deletes the word before the cursor. Useful for quick corrections.
Search through your previous Claude Code inputs. Type to filter, Enter to select.
Cycle through previously entered prompts. Works like standard shell history.
Tab completion for file paths and slash commands. Press twice to see all options.
Shows or hides the conversation side panel for quick navigation between conversations.
Cancels the current action, dismisses menus, or navigates back in the UI.
Insert a newline in your prompt without submitting. Great for writing multi-line prompts.
Use @ to mention files, URLs, or agents directly in your prompt for context.
Use # to reference specific topics or conversation tags.
Alternative way to submit your prompt, configurable via keybindings.
Opens a fresh conversation context. Previous context is preserved in history.
Resets the conversation context completely. Use when Claude seems confused or context is too long.
Summarizes the current conversation to reduce context size while retaining key information. Helps stay within token limits.
Opens the Claude Code configuration editor for adjusting settings, permissions, and preferences.
Displays token usage and cost information for the current session.
Shows a visual diff of changes Claude has made or is about to make. Helps review before accepting.
Runs diagnostic checks on your Claude Code installation to identify and fix common problems.
Cleanly exits the Claude Code CLI session.
Displays all available slash commands and their descriptions.
Creates a CLAUDE.md file with project context, conventions, and instructions for Claude to follow.
Authenticates with your Anthropic account or API key.
Signs out of the current authentication session.
Switch between available models (e.g., Sonnet, Opus, Haiku). Affects capability and cost.
Configure what actions Claude Code can perform automatically vs. requiring approval.
Fetches and displays comments from a GitHub pull request for review and response.
Asks Claude to review your current code changes, providing feedback on quality, bugs, and improvements.
Displays current session information including model, context usage, and connection status.
Sets up terminal-specific integrations like shell completions and prompt indicators.
Enables or disables vim-style keybindings for the input editor.
Switches between normal and fast output mode. Uses the same model but with faster streaming. Toggle on/off.
When enabled, automatically accepts all tool calls without prompting for permission.
Adds an additional directory to Claude's working context for multi-project workflows.
Configure and manage Model Context Protocol servers for extended tool capabilities.
Activates voice/microphone input for hands-free prompting.
Sets up integration with VS Code, JetBrains, or other supported IDEs.
The core Claude Code workflow: ask Claude to plan first, then build, review and accept changes, then plan the next step. Keeps work focused and reviewable.
Use 'think harder', 'think step by step', or 'ultrathink' to increase reasoning effort. Higher effort = better results for complex tasks but slower and more expensive.
Claude can commit, branch, rebase, resolve conflicts, and create PRs. Say 'commit this' or 'create a PR' and Claude handles the git workflow.
Tell Claude to run tests before finishing. 'Run the tests and fix any failures' ensures code actually works.
For bugs, let Claude explore the codebase first: 'Find where X happens, understand why, then fix it.' Better results than jumping straight to a fix.
Claude can edit multiple files atomically. Describe the change conceptually: 'Rename the User model to Account everywhere' and Claude finds all references.
If Claude seems confused or stuck, /clear resets context. Then re-describe what you need with fresh context.
Say 'make a React component for X' or 'make a test file for Y'. Claude creates well-structured files following project conventions.
Ask Claude to 'open the config file' or 'apply the changes we discussed'. Natural language file operations.

items.description: Get Claude to outline before coding
items.explanation: For complex tasks, say 'plan how to implement X before writing code'. Claude outlines the approach for your review before making changes.
Use --dangerously-skip-permissions or /CONCEPT to auto-accept all tool calls. Useful for trusted, well-defined tasks. Use with caution!
Use 'claude -p "prompt"' for non-interactive single-shot execution. Perfect for CI/CD pipelines and automation scripts.
Paste error messages, link files with @, or describe the architecture. More context = better results. Claude reads files you reference.
Claude automatically creates git checkpoints before major changes. Ask 'undo that' to revert to the last checkpoint.
Define skills as markdown files in .claude/skills/ or .claude/commands/. Each file becomes a slash command. Include frontmatter for name, description, and trigger conditions.
Run any custom skill by typing /skill-name. Skills can include complex multi-step workflows, coding standards, or domain-specific instructions.
Claude can launch sub-agents for parallel work. Types include: general-purpose, Explore (codebase search), Plan (architecture), code-reviewer, and more.
Launch multiple sub-agents in a single message for independent tasks. E.g., research + implementation can run in parallel for faster results.
Set run_in_background: true for agents that take a long time. You get notified when they complete. Great for large codebase searches.
Use isolation: 'worktree' to give agents their own copy of the repo. Changes stay isolated until reviewed and merged.
The Claude Agent SDK lets you build custom agents with tool use, memory, and multi-turn conversations. Available for Python and TypeScript.
Configure hooks in settings.json to run shell commands in response to Claude Code events (pre/post tool calls, prompt submit, etc.).
Use /schedule to create remote agents that run on a cron schedule. Perfect for automated code reviews, dependency updates, or monitoring.
Most common transport. MCP server communicates via stdin/stdout. Best for local tools. Config: {"command": "node", "args": ["server.js"]}
HTTP-based transport using SSE. Good for remote servers. Config: {"url": "https://mcp-server.example.com/sse"}
RESTful HTTP transport for remote MCP servers. Supports authentication headers and custom endpoints.
Place .mcp.json in your project root to define MCP servers available to all team members. Checked into version control.
Personal MCP server configuration at ~/.claude/mcp.json. Available across all projects for this user.
MCP servers can require API keys (via env vars) or OAuth flows. Configure in the server's env or headers field.
Use the /mcp slash command to add, remove, or configure MCP servers without editing JSON files directly.
Pre-built MCP servers exist for GitHub (issues, PRs), Slack (messages), Linear (tasks), Postgres (queries), and many more.
Quick-start command to create a new MCP server project with TypeScript boilerplate and example tools.
The primary way to give Claude persistent instructions. Place in project root. Contains coding conventions, architecture notes, and project-specific rules Claude should follow.
User-level instructions that apply to ALL projects. Good for personal preferences, coding style, and default behaviors.
Contains project-specific Claude Code configuration: skills, commands, settings overrides, and local state.
Defines allowed/denied tools and permissions at the project level. Checked into git for team-wide consistency.
Personal permission settings that apply across all projects. Overridden by project-level settings.
Claude Code can save memories to ~/.claude/memory/ for recall in future conversations. Includes user preferences, project context, and learned patterns.
Use @filename or @url in prompts to load file contents or web pages directly into context. Claude reads them automatically.
Export conversation history and settings for backup or migration. Import to restore on a new machine.
Set your Anthropic API key as an environment variable. Required for API-based authentication (vs. OAuth login).
Controls maximum tokens Claude can generate per response. Higher values allow longer outputs but cost more.
Route Claude Code requests through AWS Bedrock instead of direct API. Requires AWS credentials configured.
Route Claude Code requests through Google Cloud Vertex AI. Requires GCP credentials and project configured.
Prevents Claude Code from sending non-essential telemetry data. Good for privacy-sensitive environments.
Bypasses loading of CLAUDE.md instruction files. Useful for debugging when you suspect CLAUDE.md causes issues.
Controls the token budget for Claude's internal thinking/reasoning. More tokens = deeper analysis.
Override the default plugin directory location. Useful for shared team plugin repositories.
Configure external expert/knowledge lookup sources for enhanced context retrieval.
Define environment-specific settings directly in CLAUDE.md. Claude reads and applies them automatically.
In .claude/settings.json, set allowedTools to auto-approve specific tools: ["Bash(git *)", "Read", "Edit", "Write"]
Configure shell commands that run on events: PreToolUse, PostToolUse, UserPromptSubmit, etc. Great for linting, formatting, or notifications.
Launch Claude Code in interactive mode. The default way to use Claude Code — opens a conversational interface in your terminal.
Execute a single prompt and exit. Perfect for scripts, CI/CD, and automation. Output goes to stdout.
Resume the most recent conversation with full context preserved. Great for picking up where you left off.
Continue the last conversation but immediately send a new prompt. Combines -c with a new message.
Choose a specific model: --model claude-sonnet-4-6, --model claude-opus-4-6, --model claude-haiku-4-5-20251001
Pre-approve specific tools: --allowedTools 'Bash(git *)' 'Read' 'Edit'. Reduces permission prompts.
Auto-accepts every tool call without asking. Powerful but dangerous — only use for trusted, well-defined tasks in safe environments.
Output responses as JSON for programmatic consumption. Options: text, json, stream-json. Great for piping to other tools.
Shows additional debug information including tool calls, token counts, and internal reasoning steps.
Persistently set config values: claude config set model claude-opus-4-6, claude config set theme dark
Add an MCP server from command line: claude mcp add github --transport stdio -- npx @anthropic/mcp-github
Updates Claude Code CLI to the latest version. Run periodically to get new features and bug fixes.
Pipe file contents or command output directly into Claude: git diff | claude -p 'review this diff'. Stdin becomes part of the prompt context.
Stream responses as newline-delimited JSON objects. Each chunk is a valid JSON object. Best for real-time processing.
